name: The Ratline
short_description: A murdered priest. A secret list. Hunt Nazi fugitives across the globe in this gritty 1971 detective thriller. Analyze evidence, follow leads, and make sharp deductions before the trail goes cold. From the creators of Family, Rivals, Conspiracy, Echo Beach and Riley & Rochelle.
detailed_description:
Quotes

"I loved every minute of the demo"
Lucas Pope, Return of the Obra Dinn

"Delivers layers of clever puzzles and satisfying reveals all the way through to the end"

Jeremy Johnston, The Roottrees are Dead

“Roottrees fans will really enjoy sleuthing in The Ratline”
EvilTrout, The Roottrees are Dead. 

“If you love the Golden Idol games, you'll love the Ratline”
Will Ackerman, Lead Designer, Rise of the Golden Idol.

Note on Compatibility

The Ratline is not optimised for Steamdeck. Please use Mouse and Keyboard for optimal experience.

Summary

1971. A dead priest. A list of Nazi fugitives. The hunt begins.

When a Catholic priest is found murdered in Budapest, a secret list is discovered —names of Nazi war criminals who escaped justice through a covert network known as The Ratline. Now, the case is yours.

In this gritty detective thriller set in 1971, you play an investigator chasing shadows across continents. Use your wits, intuition, and network of contacts to follow leads, dig through documents, and piece together the truth.

You will:

  • Examine documents, photographs, and recovered artifacts

  • Make independent deductions with no hand-holding

  • Act like a proper sleuth, working contacts and shaking down sources from Rome to Buenos Aires

  • Hunt down your marks - put together their identities, check your work and bring them to justice.

Made by Owlskip, the studio behind acclaimed narrative detective games Family, Rivals, Conspiracy, Echo Beach, Life & Crimes, and Riley and Rochelle.

The war may be over—but the reckoning has just begun.

The Ratline

reviews.review_text:
Update 3/21

After a few days of ruminating, I wanted to add some more specific thoughts, 

One of the core information gathering tools you have is phone calls. I personally think the phone calls are totally believable. Especially the gallows humor with survivors. People have an innate need to be helpful and to fill silence, it’s what cops exploit when they’re collecting information from people. It’s also why attorneys always advise never talking to cops, you will lose every time and they’ll get what they want. This guy is a detective, it all tracks. It’s what I’d expect from people on the periphery with their own problems that have very little stake in your specific investigation. 

The plot was also very real. The main character is a ragged detective, someone’s dangled a juicy case in front of him and he doesn’t need to leave his apartment to look into it. Of course he’d bite. The story doesn’t really go into the main character’s interest beyond that other than a few mentions that he is Jewish and I think that’s ok. His primary identity here is washed up detective, not Mossad hit squad analyst. 

Ratline has about as much depth as you can get for a game of this length. It also has a lot of smoothed edges and I agree with some of the other reviewers that they can be too smooth at times (for example, not having every name you encounter listed in your options for identifying a target). 

I played on a steam deck despite the many warnings that this is not tuned for a steam deck, so I had a nonstandard experience with the UI. I found it as usable as you could expect without adding in entirely new QOL changes that are specific to playing with a controller or deck. There are many inspired UI elements that I hope other similar games pick up. The quick resize buttons for documents, search bars for documents, 3 character auto complete for searches, the  incredible rolodex available to you with 100+ phone numbers in it on Day 1, petting the dog, separating documents with two sides into two pieces of evidence, having a physical  apartment window you can open and close when you’re stuck and want something to click, being able to lower the dog’s snoring volume, having both an index of documents and a pin board, the list of options when you’re trying to label your targets name or location is hidden so you have to have some name in mind to search for and you can’t just scan the list (looking at you Golden Idol), and others I’ve probably forgotten about. 

========
I avoided playing the demo and had no intention of buying this game because there’s not much left to do with Nazis in video games (see: thousands of games with Nazis) and tbh I just didn’t think I could cope with something as depressing as Nazis getting away with having been Nazis, especially given current events. 

I was real thirsty coming off a series of deduction games and Mind diver had Riley & Rochelle, made by this same dev, included in their sources of inspiration next to Obra and Roottrees, so I caved and did the gamer thing and bought the fight the Nazis game.

If you liked Roottrees, Golden Idol, Obra, congrats you now have a 4th game to keep posting “are there more like this?” threads about everywhere.  The game is basically perfect in every way except that it is not 100 hours long.

The cases are layered and thoughtful. I was delighted by each one and never wanted to throw my steam deck through my tv because a given solution was obtuse for obtuseness’s sake. Also they are layered, like an onion. 

I strongly recommend reading the glossary as it gets updated or you’ll be a big dumb dumb like me and spend 2 hours unable to progress (which, very embarrassingly, gives you an achievement). 

It’s not perfect on steam deck, but it’s very playable with minimal squinting (I have terrible vision) and very occasional use of the magnifier. The text inputs are all “first three character” searches so that works out great for deck too! 

Lastly, the version numbers that this game uses are very cool and normal and fun and not weird.

The word that keeps coming to mind is: shallow. The entire premise of the game, hunting nazi war criminals, feels like a complete and total afterthought that has basically nothing at all to do with how cases are completed. Almost all the dialogue feels silly and tone deaf, especially considering the supposed subject of the cases. I don't really need the protagonist to be throwing out corny puns and one-liners while on the phone with a man who was kidnapped and tortured! I know it's a game, but I think showing just a tiny bit of respect for the subject matter shouldn't be too much to ask.

The cases themselves have their pros and cons for sure. I played the demo and had a blast with the first three cases. By case four, I felt like they were leaning much more into "arbitrary logic puzzle/math test word problem" and less "realistic detective procedural" that I was lead to believe this would be. I found myself totally uninterested by case five. The cases rely way too much on evidence that, quite simply, would never exist in real life and serves only to break the immersion and frustrate

Such a promising and cool concept that was largely fumbled, I'm sorry to say.

I think I'd recommend this game to any fan of Obra Dinn, Rootrees or Golden Idol series that are absolutely starved for a new investigation game and have nothing else to do. To me The Ratline was frankly a frustrating addition to the genre.

The core gameplay loop is similar to The Rootrees are Dead. You need to identify people by uncovering pieces of information using the tools at hand. However, the Rootrees had an overarching story and connection between everyone involved - the frustrating thing about The Ratline is that same connection is there, but it's buried deep below everything else. Nothing feels personal, nothing feels like a part of a bigger narrative, until it's dumped on you at the end. I think if the story could've been reframed and added more personal interactions between characters, it would make me care for it, but it didn't. The separate cases are largely uninteresting; nothing truly twisted comes up in most scenarios.

The writing isn't strong either. Aside from some funny gags, the conversations you listen in on are very stiff, and only serve to give you a name to input into a library machine or make a reference to other games. Some dialogues made me raise an eyebrow, one went something like this:
- The forger paints a very specific element on all of his fake paintings
- Okay, can you tell me what that is?
- IDK ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

But the main issue for me was the clunky interface. To look through the evidence you're forced to open up many tabs, way more than you can ever open in Golden Idol - and yet Idol cases have a button to close all tabs at once, and here you can only close them one by one by clicking a red X button, which is sometimes located at the top right corner and sometimes it doesn't. Clicking off tab doesn't close it either. The new evidence that comes up can be buried underneath a ton of paper. If you search through a library, you need to manually scroll up to see your search results. I also ran into a bunch of bugs like being unable to pin an evidence, unable to switch from a table to board scene, or pinning the wedding photograph caused the game to board to freeze, nothing could've been selected. If you put the music to 0 and restart the game, the studio logo screen still has sound. It's all very annoying.

Not to sound too negative, I think the artwork and music are good, the game has some challenging puzzles, and it stands up as it's own thing - inspired, but not derived from games that came before.

I enjoyed The Ratline and I very much hope we get some DLC cases for it. The atmosphere is wonderful and the dopamine hit of making the leap of logic that lands properly is just as strong here as for any of the other games of this type, like Obra Dinn, the Golden Idol games, or Roottrees. 

On the bad: I feel as though a few of the conclusions the user was meant to draw didn't have as strong of evidence, or as clear of a logical connection, to make it clear that this was how it was meant to be found, but the in-game hint system makes it so that you can keep progressing even when you have no idea what you're meant to say for some conclusion. I also disliked the mechanic introduced at the two-thirds point in the game: perhaps it was just my mental state while playing, but I found it difficult to figure out what to enter to get the evidence necessary for progress without guessing randomly or brute-forcing every combination I could think of.

However, what I found really lovely was, even though the game is divided into chapters or cases, the evidence you collected throughout prior chapters is retained and remains useful even into future cases, and the information and evidence you discover in the later cases recontextualizes some of what you learn later in the story. While it can become burdensome to sift through all the evidence you've collected, it does also produce some real light bulb moments when you look through an old case and find the detail that blows the whole case wide open.

I didn't think that The Ratline was better than any of the games named above, but it certainly is a fun and entertaining game, with a timely message and a healthy reminder: Power can do its best to hide evil, but it can't hide forever.

And I want my scalps.
A clever little game for people who played roottrees and want more in that style. I'm eager to see what the developer does next.
